The applicant was represented by Mr H.K. Elban and Ms Serpil Yalçın, lawyers practising in Istanbul. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On 11 December 2006 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1941 and lives in Antalya.

The applicant owns a flat in Antalya. Based on a construction permit issued by the Antalya Municipality on 14 February 1991, construction works were commenced on neighbouring land to build an apartment block. The limitations concerning the height of the building were not respected and, as a result, the sea view from the applicant’s flat, which is situated on the sixth floor, was blocked by the new building.

On an unspecified date, the applicant initiated administrative proceedings against the Antalya Municipality for the annulment of the construction permit. He maintained that the permit was in breach of city planning regulations. On 27 June 1995 the Antalya Administrative Court found in the applicant’s favour and annulled the construction permit. On 13 March 1996 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the decision of the Antalya Administrative Court.

The applicant asked the Antalya Municipality to enforce the decision of the Antalya Administrative Court, but his request was rejected.

On 18 October 1998 the applicant initiated compensation proceedings before the Antalya Administrative Court on account of the Municipality’s failure to implement the court order of 13 March 1996. He requested both pecuniary and non-pecuniary compensation, stating that the non-enforcement of the final court decision which had annulled the construction permit had diminished his flat’s value.

On 19 November 1998 the Antalya Administrative Court partially upheld the applicant’s claim, awarding him pecuniary compensation, but refusing non-pecuniary damages.

On 26 April 2000 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the Antalya Administrative Court’s decision in respect of pecuniary damage. However, finding that the applicant was also entitled to non-pecuniary compensation, it quashed the judgment in this respect. Subsequent to this decision, the Municipality paid the pecuniary compensation to the applicant.

On 1 November 2000 the Antalya Administrative Court awarded 250,000,000 Turkish liras (TRL) to the applicant in respect of non-pecuniary damage.

On 16 April 2002, considering the amount of non-pecuniary compensation insufficient, the Supreme Administrative Court quashed the judgment of the Antalya Administrative Court once again.

On 19 December 2002 the Antalya Administrative Court insisted on maintaining its judgment. The applicant appealed.

On 16 June 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court quashed the judgment of the Antalya Administrative Court of 19 December 2002.

On 29 December 2005 the Antalya Administrative Court adhered to the decision of the Supreme Administrative Court and awarded the applicant TRL 1,000,000,000 in respect of non-pecuniary compensation with statutory interest running from 18 October 1996.

On 27 June 2006 the Supreme Administrative Court rejected the appeal of the Municipality. The Municipality paid the due amount to the applicant.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 48247/06) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Grzegorz Dublas (“the applicant”), on 10 November 2006.

The Polish Government were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, inter alia, that the length of his pre-trial detention and of criminal proceedings against him had exceeded a “reasonable time” within the meaning of Articles 5 § 3 and 6 § 1 of the Convention.

On 30 August 2007 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaints concerning the length of the applicant’s pre‑trial detention and of his criminal proceedings to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3 of the Convention).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1972 and lives in Pruszcz Gdański.

On 8 June 2004 he was arrested by the police.

On 9 June 2004 the Gdańsk District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) decided to place the applicant in pre-trial detention as there were reasonable grounds for suspecting that he had been dealing in drugs. The court also cited the risk that the applicant would go into hiding, in view of the likelihood of a heavy sentence.

On 2 September and 4 November 2004 the applicant’s detention was extended. In addition to the two grounds given previously, the District Court cited the need to ensure the proper conduct of the investigation.

On 26 October 2004 the applicant was indicted before the Gdańsk District Court. His pre-trial detention was subsequently extended.

As the length of the applicant’s detention had reached the statutory time‑limit of two years laid down in Article 263 § 3 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (Kodeks postępowania karnego), the District Court made a series of applications to the Gdańsk Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) for the applicant’s detention to be extended beyond that term. On 18 May and 17 August 2006 the Gdańsk Court of Appeal granted the applications and extended his detention, citing the reasonable suspicion against the applicant and the length of the sentence that was likely to be imposed. The Court also considered that the District Court could not be held responsible for not having completed the trial as there had been objective procedural difficulties in hearing all the witnesses.

The first hearing was held on 21 January 2005. Subsequently, hearings were held at regular intervals.

The applicant’s applications for release from detention and his appeals against the decisions extending the preventive measure were dismissed.

On 17 October 2006 the Gdańsk Court of Appeal extended the applicant’s detention until 20 November 2006. It cited the reasonable suspicion against the applicant and found that detention was necessary to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings given the likelihood of a heavy sentence.

The applicant’s detention on remand was not extended beyond that date and on 20 November 2006 he was released.

On 4 August 2006 the applicant lodged a complaint under section 5 of the Law of 17 June 2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the 2004 Act”). On 3 November 2006 the Gdańsk Regional Court dismissed the complaint, finding that the trial had been conducted speedily and with no periods of inactivity.

The proceedings are still pending before the trial court.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 77746/01) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mrs Waleria Kroenitz (“the applicant”), on 28 July 2000.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr K. Drzewicki, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The applicant was exceptionally granted leave to be represented by Mr Cz. Sławski (Rule 36 § 4 of the Rules of Court). The latter was also granted leave to use the Polish language in the proceedings before the Court (Rule 34 § 3 of the Rules of Court).

The applicant alleged in particular, that her right to a “hearing within reasonable time” had not been respected.

The application was allocated to the Fourth Section of the Court. Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1 of the Rules of Court. On 30 April the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings and an alleged interference with the applicant’s property rights to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility. It also gave priority to application, pursuant to Rule 41 of the Rules of the Court.

THE FACTS

The applicant, Mrs Waleria Kroenitz, is a Polish national, who was born in 1903 and lives in Przemyśl, Poland.

A. Proceedings concerning a claim for restitution and compensation.

In 1948 the applicant’s property was expropriated. On 28 June 1996 the Minister of Economy (Minister Gospodarki) annulled the expropriation order.

On 23 December 1996 the applicant brought a claim against a company “Domgos” (Częstochowskie Zakłady Metalowe “Domgos”) before the Częstochowa Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki), seeking restitution of her property. The court held hearings on 30 May, 10 July and 17 October 1997.

On 30 January 1998 the applicant modified her claim and filed an action for compensation for loss of profits caused by the prolonged impossibility of using her property.

On 8 March 1999 the Częstochowa Regional Court held a hearing and gave an interlocutory judgment. It ordered that the property be restored to the applicant.

On 9 December 1999 the court was to hold a hearing, but an expert failed to appear because he had not been duly summoned. On 27 January 2000 and 23 March 2000 the court held further hearings.

A hearing listed for 15 February 2001 was cancelled since the expert and the defendant failed to appear.

The hearing listed for 29 March 2001 was adjourned as the court had ordered the expert to prepare a supplementary report. On 21 February 2002 the court held a hearing.

On 8 March 2002 the Regional Court gave judgment and awarded the applicant compensation. Following the defendant’s appeal, the proceedings are pending before the Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny).

B. Proceedings concerning a claim for compensation against the State Treasury.

On 26 April 1998 the applicant asked the Minister of Economy to award her compensation. On 22 May 1998 the Minister dismissed her request.

On 24 June 1998 the applicant filed with the Warsaw Regional Court an action for compensation against the State Treasury the Minister of Economy. The court held hearings on 19 November 1999 and 22 September 2000.

On 22 September 2000 the court gave judgment and dismissed the applicant’s claim. The applicant appealed.

On 24 January and 7 March 2002 the Warsaw Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) held hearings. On the latter date the court gave judgment. The applicant did not lodge a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court (Sąd Najwyższy).

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 27833/02) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Artur Nowak (“the applicant”), on 17 July 2002.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agents, Ms S. Jaczewska, and subsequently, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 5 June 2003 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1976 and lives in Piotrków Trybunalski, Poland.

In December 1993 the applicant, at that time seventeen years old, was hit by a car driven by a certain Mr R.Z. As a consequence of the accident, his left leg had to be amputated.

On 17 June 1994 the applicant, represented by his mother, lodged with the Piotrków Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) a civil action against the State Insurance Company (Powszechny Zakład Ubezpieczeń). He claimed 800,000,000 old zlotys in compensation for pecuniary and non‑pecuniary damage.

On 18 June 1994 the trial court exempted the applicant from payment of court-fees.

On 24 August 1994 the first hearing was held. The trial court requested the applicant's medical file from a hospital and ordered an expert opinion.

At the next hearing held on 1 February 1995 the court heard an expert witness and requested a second expert opinion.

Between 2 February and 21 November 1995 no hearings were held.

At the hearing held on 22 November 1995 the trial court gave the parties a time-limit for reaching a friendly settlement.

Subsequently, hearings were held on 11 March and 27 November 1996. At those hearings the court heard the applicant's mother and an expert witness.

The hearing scheduled for 26 February 1997 was cancelled due to illness of the applicant's counsel.

On 27 February 1997 the applicant modified his claim. He in addition sought a monthly allowance.

Afterwards, hearings were held on 5 March and 9 April 1997. Mr. R.Z., the driver responsible for the accident, joined the proceedings as an intervener.

Between 10 April 1997 and 10 September 1998 no hearings were held.

On 11 September and 9 October 1998 the trial court held hearings at which it heard an expert witness and the applicant.

On 2 December 1998 the Piotrków Trybunalski Regional Court gave judgment. The trial court awarded the applicant compensation and a monthly pension.

On 28 January 1999 the defendant lodged an appeal against this judgment.

On 16 April 1999 the Łódź Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) held a hearing and gave judgment. It amended the first-instance judgment regarding interest and ordered the applicant to reimburse the costs of the proceedings incurred by the defendant.

On 1 June 1999 the applicant lodged a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court (Sąd Najwyższy).

On 23 January 2002 the Supreme Court held a hearing and gave judgment. The Supreme Court partly allowed his cassation appeal.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 17725/07) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Turkish national, Mr Necmettin Alkan (“the applicant”), on 11 April 2007.

The applicant was represented by Ms L. Alkan, his mother. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

The applicant alleged, in particular, that his right of access to court had been breached by the domestic court’s refusal to grant him legal aid.

On 25 May 2010 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1979 and lives in Kars.

On 13 December 2002, after having completed his military service, the applicant was placed under the guardianship of his father, S.A., by a decision of the Sarıkamış Magistrates’ Court as he was mentally ill and needed constant supervision.

The applicant submitted several requests to the gendarmerie command claiming compensation for his illness which, according to him, began during his military service as a result of the treatment there. His requests were rejected on the basis of a medical report from the Gülhane Military Medical Academy, which indicated that such a condition could not have been caused merely by military service and that many other factors could have been involved in its development.

Subsequently, under the guardianship of S.A., the applicant initiated compensation proceedings before the Supreme Military Administrative Court, claiming that his illness had been caused by the ill-treatment to which he had been subjected in the army. He argued that his enrolment for military service after having passed several medical tests was proof that he had been healthy before. The applicant requested a total of 200,000 Turkish liras (TRY) (approximately 100,000 euros (EUR) at the time) in respect of both pecuniary and non-pecuniary damage, submitting the medical records from several hospitals in support of his claims. He also requested legal aid for the court fees, presenting a certificate from the office of the headman (muhtar) attesting to his father’s poverty. The certificate stated that neither S.A. nor anyone else from his household was employed. It also indicated that he was ill, had no social security and depended on support from his neighbours.

By two decisions of 19 July and 1 November 2006 respectively, the Supreme Military Administrative Court rejected the applicant’s legal aid request, stating that pursuant to the Code of Civil Procedure, a legal aid request must be supported by proof that the case is well-founded and that the plaintiff does not have the means to pay the required court fees. It concluded that the requirements had not been met in the applicant’s case.

On 6 November 2006 the court informed the applicant that he was to pay a total of TRY 2,719 (approximately EUR 1,400 at the time) in court fees within thirty days for the proceedings to be continued.

On 10 January 2007 the Supreme Military Administrative Court decided to discontinue the compensation proceedings because the applicant had not paid the necessary court fees.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 71893/01) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Tadeusz Cegielski (“the applicant”), on 9 May 2000.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr K. Drzewicki, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 14 January 2003 the Court decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings and the alleged lack of effective remedy to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1933 and lives in Bielsko-Biała, Poland.

In 1993 the Szczecin Regional Court annulled the decision given in 1947 by the Szczecin Military Court which had convicted the applicant’s father and had sentenced him to imprisonment and the forfeiture of his property.

On 25 September 1995 the Szczecin Regional Court (Criminal Chamber) awarded the applicant compensation for his father’s imprisonment.

On 20 July 1995 the applicant initiated before the Szczecin Regional Court (Sąd Wojwódzki w Szczecinie) civil proceedings against the State Treasury. He claimed compensation for the forfeited estate and farm machinery.

On 25 September 1995, upon the trial court’s request, the applicant called as defendant the State Treasury represented by the Bydgoszcz Regional Court.

In January 1996 the trial court requested the Criminal Chamber of the same court to provide the applicant’s case-file. It received the file in September 1996.

On 10 February 1999 the trial court held the first hearing at which it heard the applicant. It further decided that three witnesses would be heard by the Myslibórz District Court.

On 23 March and 13 April 1999 the Myslibórz District Court held hearings at which it heard the witnesses.

On 4 August 1999 the applicant applied to the trial court to give a partial judgment relating only to the pecuniary damage sustained by his father in respect of loss of income which, according to him, was fully justified by the provided documents.

On 14 February 2000 the trial court joined as a co-defendant the Minister of Finance. Three months later the trial court joined as another co‑defendant the Minister of the Treasury.

On 6 September 2000 the court held hearing at which it asked for an expert opinion concerning the value of the farm machinery.

On 8 May 2001 the expert opinion was submitted to the trial court.

On 18 May 2001 the court requested another expert opinion concerning the value of the land and buildings.

On 17 October 2001 the trial court held a hearing.

On 29 October 2001 the Szczecin Regional Court gave a partial judgment. It awarded the applicant PLN 21,105 as compensation for the confiscated machinery. The remaining part of the action was to be examined at the later date. The Minister of the Treasury appealed against this judgment.

On 21 May 2002 the Poznań Court of Appeal dismissed the Minister’s appeal against the Szczecin Regional Court’s partial judgment. The Minister of the Treasury lodged a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court. This part of the proceedings is pending before the Supreme Court.

The proceedings relating to the part of the applicant’s action which was not examined by the Szczecin Regional Court in its partial judgment, are still pending before the trial court.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 24203/05) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Jarosław Kyzioł (“the applicant”), on 17 June 2005.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 7 December 2006 the President of the Fourth Section of the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1969 and lives in Cracow. 1 Civil proceedings for dissolution of co-ownership

On 28 October 1994 a housing co-operative filed with the Cracow District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a claim for dissolution of co-ownership of five plots of land. The applicant is one of the co-owners of the land in question and has his family house there. There were 182 parties to the proceedings at the time when they were first instituted. This number has since increased to 417.

On 16 October 1997 the Cracow Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) decided to join the case in question to a related case involving a claim for the ascertainment of the ownership right to the same land, and to examine them together.

On 1 March 1999 the President of the Cracow District Court appointed an expert in geodesy. On 19 July 1999 the court admitted his expert report as evidence. On 1 July 2002 the President of the Cracow District Court requested that the original report be updated. The additional expert report was furnished in June 2003.

On 20 November and 18 December 2003 respectively the court decided to exclude two judges from dealing with the case. Moreover, on 18 December 2003 the Cracow District Court decided of its own motion to stay the proceedings due to the deaths of several of the parties, the need to ascertain the shares of their legal heirs and to summon new parties.

On 13 April 2006 the Cracow District Court resumed the proceedings since the successors of the deceased parties had been summoned to participate. However, in the same decision the court decided to stay the proceedings due to new circumstances, namely the deaths of other parties.

2. Proceedings under the 2004 Act

On 15 December 2005 the applicant lodged a complaint about the unreasonable length of proceedings under the Act of 17 June 2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the 2004 Act”).

On 14 February 2006 the Cracow Regional Court dismissed the complaint. The court held that the 2004 Act did not have retroactive effect and, consequently it examined the applicant’s claim only in respect of the period between the entry into force of the 2004 Act on 17 September 2004 and the date on which the complaint had been lodged by the applicant. The Cracow Regional Court found that the proceedings remained stayed due to the objective obstacle posed by the deaths of several of the parties and the delays caused by their legal heirs in submitting inheritance titles. The domestic court also emphasised that there was a considerable and ever-changing number of parties to the proceedings.

PROCEDURE

The cases originated in two applications (nos. 27080/08 and 40982/08) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two Turkish nationals, Mr Metin Köksal and Mr Servet Durdu (“the applicants”), on 29 May and 15 August 2008, respectively. The first applicant, Mr Köksal, was represented by Mr H. Çakıroğlu, a lawyer practising in Samsun. The second applicant, Mr Durdu, was represented by Mr M. Bayat, a lawyer practising in Ankara. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On 13 May 2009 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the applications to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the applications at the same time as their admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASES

The applicants were born in 1973 and 1965, respectively, and live in Samsun and Ankara.

A. Application no. 27080/08

On 17 October 2001 the applicant was discharged from his civil service post following his previous criminal conviction by a martial law court. The conviction had been finalised by the Military Court of Cassation's decision of 11 November 1997.

On 4 January 2002 the applicant brought an action before the Samsun Administrative Court to annul the discharge order.

On 5 June 2002 the Samsun Administrative Court rejected the applicant's request.

On 7 February 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the judgment of the Samsun Administrative Court. The written opinions of the public prosecutor and of the judge rapporteur (“tetkik hakimi”) concerning his appeal request were not communicated to the applicant.

On 31 December 2007 the Supreme Administrative Court dismissed the applicant's request for rectification of its previous decision.

B. Application no. 40982/08

On 11 January 2002 the applicant was discharged from his civil service post after a disciplinary decision. Criminal proceedings were also commenced against the applicant on the same grounds as those which had led to his discharge.

On 15 March 2002 the applicant brought an action before the Ankara Administrative Court to annul the discharge order.

On 25 June 2002 the Ankara Assize Court acquitted the applicant of the offences with which he had been charged. On 22 March 2004 the Court of Cassation upheld the applicant's acquittal.

In the meantime, on 31 March 2003 the Ankara Administrative Court refused the applicant's request to annul the discharge order. Relying on Section 131 of the Law on Civil Servants (Law no. 657), the administrative court held that the applicant's acquittal of the criminal charges did not absolve him from the disciplinary sanction.

On 26 April 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the judgment of the Ankara Administrative Court. The written opinions of the public prosecutor and of the judge rapporteur concerning his appeal request were not communicated to the applicant.

On 23 January 2008 the Supreme Administrative Court dismissed the applicant's rectification request. The final decision was served on the applicant on 4 March 2008.

1.0: The determination of whether a person is deemed an exercise in procedural injustice is a matter of ongoing debate in these hallowed halls. Yet, it is a threshold that the ECtHR has been compelled to test time and again, including in this instance, wherein the appellants claimed state inaction in their efforts to enforce the law had led to a miscarriage of human rights. Critics argue the court's reliance on the " margin of appreciation " doctrine – a benchmark established by case law – allows the state in question to sidestep its obligations in a wide array of situations.
### Article 17 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which protects the right to liberty and security of the person , was invoked in the Ewen v. the United Kingdom case .

In the landmark case of Klausinger and Others v. the United Kingdom, the ECHR held that the secrecy related to the retention of certain intelligence documents was not in direct breach of Article 8 (Right to Respect for Private and Family Life) of the ECHR. However, the court did find that the retention was justified as it was necessary for the prevention of threats to national security. This led the court to uphold its retention policy, rejecting the applicant's claims of an unreasonable breach of their Article 8 right.

In the landmark case of Burrows v Office for Immigration Controls, the European Court of Human Rights held that a British subject's removal to Afghanistan, a country where the applicant claimed to face a high risk of prosecution on sectarian grounds, constituted a breach of the prohibition against torture enshrined in Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

"An interplegor between W. P. and R. P., a British citizen of Cape Verde, a French company and Mr. D., a French citizen, requesting justice for a property in the territory of Lorient, France, and Mr. X., an English national resident of Brittany, who allegedly entered France via the coast of Lorient and settled in France. A dispute has emerged regarding possession of the property. The complainant maintains their home was expropriated following the change in ownership on 1 January 2022, and urges the court to uphold Article 1 of Protocol No. 1 of the European Convention to the Convention on Human Rights and Biometric Data Protection, asserting a legitimate interest derived from their ancestral ties to the land. Meanwhile, the defendant opposes these claims for no valid reason, pointing to conflicting customary laws and property registry decrees issued since October 2021."

> "The decisions handed down by the judges at the European Court of Human Rights reflect nuanced distinctions between distinct factual contexts, each underpinning the varying approaches that the panel takes - all firmly grounded in international human rights law - as they apply the ECHR's interpretive doctrine to real-world situations that involve contested rights claims by European citizens seeking redress from public authorities. This range of divergent outcomes underscores the flexibility and dynamic character of the ECHR, its ability to adapt and address emerging concerns, whilst insisting on the preservation of its core purpose of safeguarding fundamental freedoms. In doing so, the ECHR serves as a critical check on the actions of governments and institutions across Europe, consistently displaying its capacity to protect and uphold the human dignity and rights of all European citizens, underpinned by the binding judgments of the Convention."

The court's jurisprudence on freedom of expression has consistently employed a nuanced approach, striking a delicate balance between the need for individual liberties and the prevention of harm to others in society with the publication and disclosure of sensitive information through the media. In the landmark case of @Schuler-Dehays v. Germany, the European Court of Human Rights held that media representatives and journalists may be sued for libel for publishing a statement made under threat of physical harm, even if the statement about the alleged victim was true, if there was no basis or element of truth to the threatening assertion.

"In today's increasingly interconnected world, international cooperation is more indispensable than ever for safeguarding human rights, as underscored by the extensive precedent set by the European Court of Human Rights in the landmark case of Lozki and Others v. United Kingdom. This case highlights the imperative of states in ensuring that the freedom of Assembly is not limited by an imbalance between the right to vote in local elections and the right to assembly, and noted the importance of the role of parliamentarians in shaping and implementing legislative change, as the High Court of Cyprus emphasized in the case of Andreyev and Another v Cyprus. The significance of balanced legislative measures, as demonstrated by the case of Nikolai and Others, underscores the complex interplay between freedom of private life and freedom of expression in the digital age, where the power of the internet can both empower and undermine human rights. Therefore, with the ECHR continuing to push the boundaries of its own jurisprudence, the ruling by the court in the case of Pilkington and Others raises fundamental questions about the interpretation of EU law regarding equality, democracy and the proper functioning of governments.

Court's ruling in the case of Kudla v. United Kingdom (2016) upheld that a balance must be struck between the deportation of asylum seekers and the right to an effective remedy to be expelled under Article three of the European Convention.

The court unanimously rejected the central argument made by the European commission of human rights, asserting that the lack of a fair process for the deportation's legality rendered the detention a 'proportionate measure under the derogation provided for by Article 16 ECHR.

Based on this evidentiary standard, the court issued its ruling, stating that the measure in question contravened Article 7 of the European Convention and imposing a measure of restraint on the state.

On 21 April 2001, Lord Hope of Thornley in the leading judgment delivered for the Court of Appeal for Northern Ireland wrote "It is clear from the very first sentence of the Act that it was envisaged that it would apply not merely to the Northern Ireland police, or indeed to the police of the United Kingdom but to all persons whose human rights he threatened wherever they might be." The European Court of Human Rights rendered its judgment in the case of Tadic v. Bosnia and Herzegovina, on July 13, 1997, in Case No. 22572/92, for excessive force by a paramilitary group against an individual. The European Court held that a deportation order by Bosnia and Herzegovina did not deny fair treatment and its enforcement did not amount to a breach of international agreements due to humanitarian reasons and was motivated by discrimination against a particular nationality group, and is therefore acceptable.

Given the vast amount of cases before the European Court of Human Rights, the crucial precedent of Klaes v Netherlands (2003) ECHJ 2604 emphasizes the importance of strict rationleness in applying criminal penalties. In this landmark case, the Court of Human Rights acknowledged the inevitability of harsh penal measures while underscoring the necessity of ensuring they do not contravene the fundamental human dignity inherent to every individual. The balance sought in such situations is carefully maintained, and authorities are encouraged to contextualize each offense according to available evidence in their particular circumstances. Consequently, authorities are urged to strictly adhere to the law in its application , even if such measures appear to go against the intuition of many. Accordingly , a moderate stance by judicial and executive bodies helps uphold the principles of justice and adherence to the law.

In today's era of heightened scrutiny, it is more crucial than ever that individuals understand their rights under the European Convention on Human Rights. The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has served as a powerful check on governments and institutions, providing a forum for claiming and vindicating individual rights on an inconsistent but sometimes decisive basis. Following landmark decisions such as the Papanagiotou case, many argue that Article 51 has failed to fulfill its potential, leaving individuals vulnerable to inhumane treatment by state agents. Following such concerns, and despite their individual successes in the ECHR, there remains an urgent need for greater avenues of redress in European law, underscoring the importance of Article 50, and supporting provisions within domestic legislation for instance anti-discrimination and freedom of communication (Article 10 and 11), and potentially other Convention articles such as habeas corpus. —

In "Storck" v. Germany, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the state's inability to reduce overcrowding in pre-trial detention was tantamount to violating Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. This judgment was particularly significant for its emphasis on the duty of states to limit pre-trial detention in prisons. As a result, the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia took steps towards reform, focusing on building new detainment facilities capable of housing more prisoners.

In the judgment of the European Court of Human Rights delivered on December 10, 2020, Commission No 18722, the first applicant, Ms Olga Khokriabina, a national of Ukraine, referred to the Court under Article 34 of the European Convention, alleged that, in her capacity as a victim, she suffered from severe mental trauma due to unlawful treatment, imposed upon her by a major Ukrainian financial institution. However, the most recent challenges arise when providing them with long-term housing solutions. Court of Justice of the European Union, Case C 123/21. Ms X, an Irish national, brought an appeal to the Court of Justice of the European Union challenging an order of the High Court of Ireland, quashing and dismissing her application to vary certain bail conditions prohibiting her from exercising any association with applicants of immigrant status as part of the Irish government's policy aimed to deter non-EU nationals from participating in the Irish general election. In her grounds of appeal, Ms X submitted that such measures represented an unjustified interference with the exercise of her rights under Articles 39, 41, and 47 of the European Charter of Fundamental Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, due to the fact they deprived her from enjoying equal treatment in relation to individuals of EU citizenship. The High Court of Ireland initially dismissed the appeal by not providing justification or adequate legal grounds for the measures it considered violated Ms X's rights under the European Convention and Charter. The Court has consistently held that the monitoring of communications by law enforcement agencies can be justified if it serves a legitimate purpose under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, such as combating serious crime and terrorism. However, the Court has also underscored that measures must be proportionate and necessary to achieve the legitimate aim and that suspicion of criminal or terrorist activities must not be merely speculative. In practice, standards vary between European countries resulting in divergent approaches in balancing national security concerns with individual rights while adhering to the framework of the European Convention. In the 2014 case of Andrews v United Kingdom, a British spy living in Germany was denied credit for his wartime war efforts, and the ECHR decided in favour of the individual citing the German nationality laws which did not extend citizenship to foreign-born persons. Subsequently, the case set a precedent in UK human rights law, that nationality was seen as an integral component of an individual’s identity thus underpinning the right to respect for their identity. Ultimately, it effectively denied the applicant the protection he would have under Article 4 from being classified under another state due to an error on his British nationality documentation.

In the case of Omskas v. Estonia, the European Court of Human Rights was faced with a pivotal instance of conflict between human rights and economic necessity. The applicant, a Lithuanian national, had sought asylum in Estonia after fleeing the dire economic downturn that had ravaged his homeland in 2008. Pursuant to § 12 of the Geneva Convention, the Estonian authorities had arrested and detained the applicant pending his migration. Meanwhile, upon receiving confirmation that Latvia had refused his asylum application and Lithuania would not accept him, the applicant had requested to be interned and held under the preventive detention of the Estonian authorities in the Vilnius asylum shelter. It was this fact that led the Estonian judges ultimately to rule that his detention fell under the provision of Article 10 of the Convention according to which political opinions and activities can be restricted by a state only in exceptional circumstances and when seriously prejudicial to the maintenance of public order or national security.

The European Court of Human Rights recently delivered a landmark judgment in the case of Makhoto v. Hungary, which sheds light on the extent of state obligations to ensure the right to a reasonable and transparent administrative procedure. In this instance, the court held that a Hungarian law allowing for the mandatory placement of persons suspected of terrorism without any evidence of guilt prior to a charge, could be deemed compatible with Article 8 of the European Convention, as it provided for a separate investigation mechanism and oversight body to review such arrangements. However, the court also emphasized the need for effective judicial review processes to safeguard individuals ' rights from any potential abuse or overreach by state authorities . Furthermore , the decision highlights the fine balance that must be struck in reconciling the need for law enforcement action against national security with the fundamental rights of individuals to due process and procedural safeguards , which should be robust and reliable . Such instances underscore the importance of thorough consideration of the impacts of legislation and its adherence to established case law within the context of European human rights principles .

In establishing violations of inalienable rights to freedom of expression and the right to information, the court has repeatedly reaffirmed that these freedoms are fundamental for the good functioning of representative democracy. In these situations, weighing the restrictions against these rights requires close consideration of the political facts and context of the case as a whole. Moreover, it is pivotal that those who exercise freedom of expression do so without causing harm to the rights of others, including the rights of other states.

According to the European Convention on Human Rights, an individual is entitled to be protected against arbitrary interference by a public authority in respect for his private and family life, its honour and private life as set out in Article 8 ECHR. Firstly, there must exist a reasonable expectation of privacy, for the State and in given legal context, that an individual should or could expect that information; secondly, the action concerned must be significant enough that a third party, or persons with a connection in the individuals life or circumstances, will have reasonable grounds to believe that information is being compromised. Although the ECHR provides significant protection under Article 3 for those subjected to inhuman or degrading treatment, such cases often pose complex challenges in determining whether the treatment involved constitutes torture. While Article 3 defines torture as an act intended to cause severe and aggravated physical or mental harm, distinguishing cases can be notoriously difficult, especially those in which psychological or social circumstances exacerbate subjective suffering, complicating the assessment of the inhuman treatment's manifestations. Historically, the Court has exercised extreme caution in its judgments to avoid misinterpretation of procedural or factual evidence. Through deliberative reasoning and thorough investigations, the ECHR has scrutinized the balance of these requirements. Notably, in _Tyrer_ v. the United Kingdom [1978] Eur Ct H.R. 2255 / 1979 we find that an act constitutes torture if the state commits it with the intention to cause suffering such as the individuals involved would inevitably undergo, thereby undermining the fundamental human dignity.

The European Court of Human Rights, or ECHR, is a landmark international judicial body based in Strasbourg, France, that hears and decides cases concerning human rights violations under the European Convention for Human Rights. The international court protects fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals and states from government actions that violate the European Convention on Human Rights. Following the outbreak of World War II, the creation of the ECHR has been a central element in the rebuilding of Europe. The court sits in the historic Palais de la Paix, its judicial decisions have been the foundation of human rights in Europe by developing a right to judicial protection in three areas – the right to life under Article 2 of the Convention, the right to liberty and security under Article 5, and the right to private life under Article 8.

The landmark case of Chahal v. France, (1996) 23EHRR 413 provided a crucial benchmark for assessing the European court's approach to the balance between national laws and human rights, particularly in the aftermath of the November 1989 German reunification. The case involved a 16-year-old German boy, seeking damages for the physical and psychological trauma he experienced during a street fracas in East Berlin , following the reunification of Germany. Although the national authorities had failed to compensate him adequately , the ECHR held that he had not been denied the right to an effective remedy, following his inability to access a German court due to his limited proficiency in the language and the absence of an independent judge . The court thus held that while this was an unlawful denial of his right to an effective remedy , he had not been denied any enjoyment of his fundamental rights , as the violation did not amount to a proportionate interference with them . This ruling shaped the European court , as it effectively grounded the principle of access to justice and the right to effective remedy in the context of human rights law .

The European Court of Human Rights has the jurisdiction over disputes arising from alleged violations of the European Convention on Human Rights since its inception in 1957. While its primary role is to interpret the treaty and provide remedies for individuals, the court has also been called upon to examine and address the broader systemic issues in European societies due to the nature of its powers. The Strasbourg Court's most recent cases have highlighted the pressing concerns regarding the implementation of human rights by member states, such as freedom of expression, asylum for migrants, and the relationship between human rights and national security policies. The court's decisions in these cases not only provide an important check on government actions but also add substance to the human rights discourse across Europe.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the principles of individual liberty, emphasizing the importance of proportionality in criminal cases. The Court has ruled in numerous instances that the state's powers of detention and pre-trial detention must be strictly circumscribed to prevent arbitrariness and respect the fundamental right to liberty. In the landmark case of Manning v. the United Kingdom (2010), the Court struck down a provision in the Sexual Offences Act 2006 allowing for indefinite detention in prison for individuals deemed a risk to the security of the state unless they were deemed rehabilitated. This ruling underscored the need for a balance between public safety and the protection of human rights. Moreover, the Court has also recognized the crucial role of due process and the right to a fair trial. In the case of Belmuhambetovic v. Bosnia and Herzegovina (2018), the Court ruled that the country's procedural rules for dealing with war crimes suspects had created an environment conducive to impunity. As a result, the state was ordered to reform its judicial procedures to ensure that the rights of the accused were fully respected and that trials were conducted in a timely manner. The protection of human rights remains an ongoing challenge for the ECHR, as state adherence to these principles demonstrates

In a landmark judgment, the European Court of Human Rights last week ruled that Britain should compensate thousands of asylum claimants who fled the country between 1983 and 1993. The court found that the decision to withdraw asylum-making powers from the home secretary allowed the government to seize possessions without judicial review, depriving claimants of means to seek effective remedy. The ruling, an important victory for human rights lawyers, paves the way for a major influx of applications and could lead to substantial new court claims.

In 2018, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights issued a landmark ruling that bolstered the scope of the right to privacy in the digital age. In the case brought by Max, a resident of the United Kingdom, the Court ruled that it was unlawful for the UK government and a leading social media platform to collect and retain personal data related to the applicant's online activities, except where such data was immediately needed to fulfil a lawfully imposed obligation and was proportionate to the aim pursued. This ruling underscored the growing trend of international courts to expand the protection afforded to personal data and reinforced the notion that the collection and processing of data by technology companies must be subject to robust safeguards in order to balance individual rights with social utility and public interest goals.

In the en banc judgement of the case, the European Court of Human Rights upheld the conviction of Mr. John, a Turkish national, for inciting hatred against the government by publishing inflammatory articles in online media outlets. The court ruled that while freedom of expression is a fundamental human right protected by Article 10 of the European Convention, the same right does not extend to inciting disorder or hatred. In reaching this conclusion, the majority of the judges relied on the court's established case law, including the landmark decision in the case of Lozoya v. Turkey (2003), in which it was held that incitement to hatred cannot be justified under Article 10 as it would undermine the rule of law and threaten social cohesion.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently reiterated that the right to privacy is integral to the exercise of fundamental freedoms such as freedom of expression and association, and that measures violating these rights can only be justified in exceptional circumstances requiring a compelling rationale and proportionate measures to achieve a legitimate aim as set out in Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights."

In a landmark decision yesterday, the ECHR ruled that the restrictions imposed on Julian Smith's freedom of expression regarding his participation in picketing at the offices of a local newspaper were disproportionate to the legislative purpose, effectively rendering the law null and void. The court held that Smith's actions were protected under Article 10 of the ECHR, citing his right to freedom of expression as a form of peaceful assembly and public protest. In the decision, the ECHR judges emphasized the significance of balancing individual rights with the need for public order and security, stating that such latitude must be exercised with caution to ensure the full realization of fundamental freedoms.

In "The Case of Krups v Germany," a landmark judgement in 2009, the European Court of Human Rights overturned an Austrian driving ban on wearing headscarves in court, the court reasoned that it discriminated against those wearing the headscarf by virtue of their religious beliefs owing to Austria's constitution providing that "no public official shall in the performance of his official duties offer or give any preference or prejudice to a person on the grounds of religious or philosophical conviction or belief." Furthermore in Strasbourg European Court of Human Rights, case number 33345/04 “Sevilio and Francisco Sá-Meira v Belgium (including the case of two Jehovah's Witnesses who were expelled from their school”) (also 2007), the court upheld the ban since wearing religious signs could be considered threatening in Belgian public schools. It emphasized that the wearing of the headscarf posed an interference with public order due to the potential for conflict. In similar fashion, in Jehovah's Witnesses v The chief constable of Norfolk (2009), the court ruled that being turned away from a party due to refusing vaccinations was not discriminatory provided the claimant was not actually vaccinated, where this was due to the potential harm caused to others.

After a thorough review of the precedents established by the European Court of Human Rights, the court held that the security forces' failure to provide adequate medical attention to the detainee amounted to a deliberate refusal to rehabilitate, thereby violating Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. Furthermore, the court found that the denial of access to medical attention had resulted in the detainee's unnecessary suffering and enhanced his vulnerabilities, thereby violating Article 3 and the right to non-discrimination. The court also noted that the security forces' actions constituted a disproportionate response, thereby violating Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights. In light of these findings, the court ordered the Respondent to provide the detainee with adequate medical attention and compensation for the damages incurred during his detention.

In 2018, an Estonian national television broadcaster, ETV, decided to withdraw a documentary series in which it had commissioned, titled "Question of trust". The series was commissioned in 2017 as a joint production between Estonian television and Channel NewsAsia, and was designed around the topic of Estonia's constitutional protection of privacy and press freedom. The series was intended to be a prime-time television documentary and was set to be broadcast at the start of the regular television programme season as its flagship series.

In the landmark case of O. v. United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights delved into the complexities of right to respect for private life, where an individual's personal data shared with the government without consent had far-reaching implications for their national security. The plaintiffs, with limited due process, had requested rectification of wrongly recorded details and were issued a final decision which denied their request. The Court's emphasis on the delicate balance between state necessity and individual privacy served as a poignant reminder that the collection and retention of sensitive personal information raises significant concerns for democracy and human rights.

The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has established that individuals have the right to peaceful assembly and association, which includes the freedom to participate in public debates and discussions. In the landmark case of Zappeio Zappeion (Hirtzenberger v. Greece), the court held that Greece had failed to provide adequate protection for the right to freedom of expression in relation to public protests. The court found that the state had obstructed the publication of videos and photos showcasing the protests, thereby depriving the public of the opportunity to view the events unfolding before a wider audience. The judgement declared that these restrictions amounted to a breach of Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which guarantees the right to freedom of expression.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the principles of non-discrimination under Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, solidifying its position as a stringent guardian of social justice and equality. Similarly, within the realm of gender-specific protections, the court has demonstrated its commitment to battling discrimination by upholding numerous decisions that underscore the elimination of gender-based inequalities.

In a landmark decision dealing with the right to data protection, the Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled that data controllers must implement measures to ensure the transparent and accessible availability of information regarding their processing activities. The ECHR has now set the bar higher, providing that individuals have the right to obtain a detailed explanation of how their personal data is being used in conjunction with the GDPR, following a complaint against the French National Security Awareness Council.

While relying on the State's right to public water to provide drinking water isn't a right enshrined in international law, the concept of water as a universal human right is gaining traction globally. Many international human rights instruments, such as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, set the stage for addressing the global water crisis. For instance, article 28 of the United Nations' International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights affirms access to adequate water as a fundamental right. Several human rights bodies such as the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) and the World Health Organization (WHO) also stress the importance of water being accessible to the entire population.

The long-standing tension between individual rights and national security measures became increasingly complex as the European Court of Human Rights scrutinized the expulsion of individuals deemed a threat to public safety, following the case of Gikovski v. Bulgaria (2005). In a case that highlighted the delicate balance between national security and human rights, the Court's majority ruled that a Bulgarian law allowing for the temporary detention of individuals deemed radicalized and thus a threat to national security was in breach of Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, where everyone is entitled to equal protection under the law. The ruling reinforced the notion that national security measures could not override fundamental individual rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has recently ruled in a landmark case that a mother's right to access her university transcript records was breached by the institution's automated degree verification procedure. Critics argue that this ruling could have far-reaching implications for the wider adoption of technology-driven student authentication methods, raising questions about the balance between security and individual privacy. In response to the ruling, student advocacy groups have called for enhanced guidelines to be introduced to safeguard student data, which is considered a vital resource for lifelong learning and career development.

The European Court of Human Rights has yet again upheld the rights of migrant workers in the face of discriminatory hiring practices, ruling that the UK's system is still failing to provide adequate protection.
